revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:二酸化炭素泉]]


'''Carbon Dioxide Springs''' (二酸化炭素泉) is a classification of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|qid=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}<nowiki/>water quality based on the displayed spring quality names. It falls under the category of {{ill|therapeutic hot springs|ja|温泉#療養泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic hot springs|1=Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q655311}}

== Overview ==
{{Referencesの明記|section=1|date=2023-3|qq=}}
[[File:Drinking glass 00118.gif|right|thumb|A glass filled with {{ill|Carbonated water|en|Carbonated water|qid=Q264554|lt=carbonated water|1=Carbonated water}}A hot spring or {{ill|Mineral spring|en|Mineral spring|qid=Q1365924|lt=Mineral spring|1=Mineral spring}}<nowiki/>that contains at least 1,000 mg of free {{ill|Carbonic acid|en|Carbonic acid|qid=Q104334|lt=carbonic acid|1=Carbonic acid}}
({{ill|Carbon dioxide|en|Carbon dioxide|qid=Q1997|lt=carbon dioxide|1=Carbon dioxide}}) per kilogram of water. According to the {{ill|Guidelines for Mineral Spring Analysis|ja|鉱泉分析法指針|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guidelines for Mineral Spring Analysis|qq=}}
, if the concentration is below 1,000 mg, it is not recognized as a therapeutic spring, but if it contains at least 250 mg, it is still classified as a hot spring. The bathing temperature is considered to be around 37.5°C because the perceived temperature is 2-3°C higher than that of ordinary water. The old classification for this type of spring was '''Simple Carbonic Acid Spring''' (単純炭酸泉), and even today, the term '''Carbonic Acid Spring''' (炭酸泉) is widely used. In some cases, it is colloquially called "{{ill|Ramune|en|Ramune|qid=Q1206045|lt=Ramune|1=Ramune}}
" water.<ref>[http://www.lamune-onsen.co.jp/ Lamune Onsen] (Official website of Lamune Onsen)</ref>.
The solubility of carbon dioxide decreases as the water temperature rises. According to {{ill|Henry's law|en|Henry's law|qid=Q285804|lt=Henry's law|1=Henry's law}}
, the saturation solubility at different temperatures is as follows:
37.5°C - approximately 1,350 mg/L (optimal temperature)
40°C - approximately 1,050 mg/L
Thus, the concentration of carbon dioxide varies significantly with the spring temperature. Some springs form visible bubbles of carbon dioxide, while others contain dissolved carbonic acid or {{ill|bicarbonate ion|ja|炭酸水素イオン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bicarbonate ion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 炭酸水素塩|1=Bicarbonate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q409202}}
, making the bubbles invisible.
The extent of bubble formation varies by spring source. However, the actual solubility and the relationship between the bubbles and the effectiveness of the spring cannot be generalized.<ref group="note">Even if carbon dioxide is dissolved in water, its behavior changes depending on coexisting substances and temperature. The effects of the spring also vary depending on its composition.</ref> Additionally, even if a spring contains a high amount of carbon dioxide, it may not necessarily be classified as a pure Carbon Dioxide Spring (Simple Carbon Dioxide Spring). In Japan, it is often categorized as a {{ill|bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}<nowiki/>or an {{ill|Chalybeate|en|Chalybeate|qid=Q3046709|lt=Chalybeate springs|1=Chalybeate springs}}, depending on its composition. Chalybeate springs often appear {{ill|reddish-brown|ja|赤褐色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reddish-brown|1=Reddish Brown|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11635977}}
. Some springs possess both the qualities of a Carbon Dioxide Spring and a Bicarbonate Spring.
== Comparison of 11 and Old Spring Classifications ==
The following table shows the comparison between new and old classifications:
{| class="wikitable"
|+ Comparison of 11 and Old Spring Classifications<ref>{{Cite web|和書
|title=参考資料２新旧泉質名対照
|url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf
|format=PDF
|publisher=環境省自然環境局自然環境整備課温泉地保護利用推進室
|accessdate=2018-03-26
}}
</ref>
! scope="col" id="旧泉質名" | Old Spring Name
! scope="col" id="新泉質名" | 11 Spring Name
! scope="col" id="略記泉質名" | Abbreviated Spring Name
|-
| Simple Carbonic Acid Spring (単純炭酸泉) || Simple Carbon Dioxide Spring (単純二酸化炭素泉) || Simple CO<sub>2</sub> Spring (単純CO<sub>2</sub>泉)
|}
== Effects ==
{{See also|Carbonated_water#Health_effects}}
Although these effects are not guaranteed for everyone, the following benefits are attributed to this type of spring:
=== Bathing ===
In addition to {{ill|general hot spring benefits|ja|一般的適応症|1=General Indications for hot spring use|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/general hot spring benefits|13=User:Immanuelle/General Indications for hot spring use|qq=}}
, it is said to help with {{ill|Hypertension|en|Hypertension|qid=Q41861|lt=high blood pressure|1=Hypertension}}
, {{ill|Arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|qid=Q184559|lt=arteriosclerosis|1=Arteriosclerosis}}
, {{ill|cuts|ja|切り傷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 切創|1=cut|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4372969}}
, and {{ill|Burn|en|Burn|qid=Q170518|lt=burns|1=Burn}}

=== Drinking ===
It is believed to aid in chronic {{ill|digestive system disorders|ja|消化器病|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/digestive system disorders|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消化器学|1=Gastroenterology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q120569}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|Constipation|en|Constipation|qid=Q178436|lt=constipation|1=Constipation}}.
== Natural Carbon Dioxide Springs ==
[[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|thumb|Lamune Onsenkan (Nagayu Onsen)]]
Carbon Dioxide Springs are hot springs with a high concentration of dissolved carbon dioxide, which are rare worldwide. The {{ill|Nagayu Onsen|ja|長湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagayu Onsen|qq=}} area, home to {{ill|Lamune Onsenkan|ja|ラムネ温泉館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lamune Onsenkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135551122|1=Lamune Onsenkan}}, has an abundance of natural Carbon Dioxide Springs.
There are relatively few natural Carbon Dioxide Springs in Japan. Even fewer are pure Carbon Dioxide Springs that do not contain {{ill|Sodium bicarbonate|en|Sodium bicarbonate|qid=Q179731|lt=sodium bicarbonate|1=Sodium bicarbonate}} or {{ill|salt|ja|食塩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/salt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩|1=Salt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11254}}
. One example is {{ill|Shiramizu Mineral Spring|ja|白水鉱泉|q=Mineral spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiramizu Mineral Spring|qq=}} in Ōita Prefecture, which is a nearly pure Simple Carbon Dioxide Spring. Another is Nagayu Onsen, which has both Simple Carbon Dioxide Springs and Bicarbonate Springs.
Japan has few Carbon Dioxide Springs due to its high volcanic activity, which results in high-temperature springs where carbon dioxide is less soluble. Outside Japan, there are many Carbon Dioxide Springs in {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|qid=Q46|lt=Europe|1=Europe}}
, especially in {{ill|Germany|en|Germany|qid=Q183|lt=Germany|1=Germany}}
, where well-known hot spring resorts such as {{ill|Baden-Baden|en|Baden-Baden|qid=Q4100|lt=Baden-Baden|1=Baden-Baden}} and {{ill|Bad Nauheim|en|Bad Nauheim|qid=Q16105|lt=Bad Nauheim|1=Bad Nauheim}}
exist.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.co2spa.com/about/ |title=What is a Carbon Dioxide Spring? |publisher=Mitsubishi Chemical Aqua Solutions |accessdate=2021-09-12 |qq=}}
</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.co2spa.com/history/ |title=History of Carbon Dioxide Springs |publisher=Mitsubishi Chemical Aqua Solutions |accessdate=2021-09-12 |qq=}}
</ref>.
=== Notable Hot Springs in Japan ===
* {{ill|Nagayu Onsen|ja|長湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagayu Onsen|qq=}}<ref name="Yamamura_47_h_p7">Yamamura Junji, ''47 Prefectures: Hot Spring Encyclopedia'', p.7, Maruzen Publishing, 2015, ISBN 978-4-621-08996-5</ref> (Ōita Prefecture)
* {{ill|Funagoya Onsen|ja|船小屋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagoya Onsen|qq=}}<ref name="Yamamura_47_h_p7" /> (Fukuoka Prefecture)
== Artificial Carbon Dioxide Springs ==
{{See also|{{ill|Artificial Hot Spring|ja|人工温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Artificial Hot Spring|qq=}}}}

Carbon dioxide gas from cylinders can be used to create artificial Carbon Dioxide Springs. For example, in 1997, a subsidiary of {{ill|Mitsubishi Rayon|en|Mitsubishi Rayon|qid=Q6883134|lt=Mitsubishi Rayon|1=Mitsubishi Rayon}}<nowiki/>developed a device that dissolves at least 1,000 ppm (1 g of carbon dioxide per liter) into hot water. In 2014, a small company in {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|qid=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}
developed a device that instantly saturates hot water with up to 1,300 ppm of carbon dioxide. As a result, some {{ill|super sentō|ja|スーパー銭湯|1=Super Sentō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/super sentō|13=User:Immanuelle/Super Sentō|qq=}}
facilities have started offering "artificial carbonated springs" as a highlight. Studies have shown that high-concentration carbonated baths improve blood circulation and reduce the risk of lower limb amputation in patients with {{ill|Peripheral artery disease|en|Peripheral artery disease|qid=Q378681|lt=peripheral artery disease|1=Peripheral artery disease}}
.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://co2sg.com/rule.htm |title=Artificial Carbon Dioxide Spring Research Association |publisher=Artificial Carbon Dioxide Spring Research Association |accessdate=2021-09-12 |qq=}}
</ref>.
== References ==
<references group="note" />{{Reflist|qq=}}
